title: Cropped 1 July 2026: Heatwave scorches Europe | UK 2050 farm plan | What’s next for the High Seas Treaty - Carbon Brief
gist: A fortnightly land-food-nature digest covering heat damage to European agriculture and livestock, the UK's 2050 farming roadmap and revived anti-deforestation rules, EU approval of gene-edited crops, and the path for implementing the High Seas Treaty ahead of its January 2027 COP1.

themes.theme: Heat hits agriculture and food supply

insights.claim: At least several hundred thousand poultry perished in France due to extreme heat, while cows and pigs suffered heat stress in Belgium, raising concerns about milk and meat production.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Direct livestock losses translate into near-term supply tightness and price risk in European poultry, dairy and meat markets.

insights.claim: UK government data obtained by Carbon Brief showed twice as many animals died from heat stress en route to slaughterhouses in record-hot 2025 versus 2024.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Rising in-transit livestock mortality signals mounting cost and welfare-regulation risk for meat supply chains as heatwaves recur.
insights.quote: twice as many animals died due to heat stress en route to slaughterhouses

insights.claim: Prolonged high temperatures place huge stress on livestock, dry out soils and reduce crop resilience, all while putting more pressure on nature, according to Nature Friendly Farming Network chief Martin Lines.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Compounding heat stress on soils and crops is a structural drag on European agricultural productivity and input costs.
insights.quote: Prolonged high temperatures place huge stress on livestock, dry out soils and reduce crop resilience, all while putting more pressure on nature.

insights.claim: Fruit growers across Utah are reporting near-total harvest losses after a warm, dry winter and record-breaking spring heatwave drove early bloom, followed by a series of April freezes.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Whiplash between early bloom and late freezes is an emerging crop-loss pattern that raises US specialty-crop and orchard insurance risk.

insights.claim: The UK government launched a 2050 farming roadmap for England aimed at climate resilience, higher domestic food production and nature recovery, plus 47m pounds for peatland restoration, but the NFU said it falls short on action and delivery.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A long-horizon farm plan light on delivery detail leaves UK agricultural transition funding and direction uncertain for the sector.

insights.claim: The UK revived anti-deforestation rules requiring companies to check supply chains of soya, palm oil, cocoa and rubber are free from illegal land clearances, initially targeting illegal deforestation before moving to a blanket deforestation-free standard.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: New UK supply-chain deforestation rules add compliance costs and traceability demands for commodity importers, mirroring repeatedly delayed EU plans.

insights.claim: Ed Miliband was poised to announce a 400m pound commitment to the Tropical Forest Forever Facility, but it was shelved over optics concerns amid a bitter row over defence spending.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Defence-spending pressure crowding out forest finance signals fiscal squeeze on UK climate commitments, a headwind for forest-protection funding flows.

themes.theme: EU crops, methane and biodiversity disclosure

insights.claim: The European parliament approved rules allowing the use of gene-edited plants, marking a major shift in the EU's approach to modified crops.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: EU acceptance of gene-edited crops opens a large regulated market for agri-biotech and seed-technology firms previously blocked in Europe.

insights.claim: An analysis of 120 of the world's largest listed companies found they disclose roughly two-thirds of assessed climate information but less than one-twentieth of the equivalent biodiversity information.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A vast corporate biodiversity-disclosure gap flags coming regulatory and data-provider opportunity as nature reporting catches up to climate reporting.
insights.quote: companies disclose roughly two-thirds of assessed climate information, yet less than one-20th of the equivalent biodiversity information

insights.claim: Growing oil crops such as oil palm and coconuts potentially caused the long-term loss of 1.5% of global plant and animal species between 1995 and 2020, with the largest impacts in the tropics, per a Nature Food study.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Quantified biodiversity cost of oil-crop expansion strengthens the case for deforestation-linked supply-chain scrutiny of palm and coconut commodities.

insights.claim: Only around 1% of the high seas are currently protected despite covering half the planet, and the High Seas Treaty (BBNJ) provides the first-ever legal framework to protect them from shipping, fishing, pollution and climate pressure, per High Seas Alliance director Rebecca Hubbard.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A new legal regime for two-thirds of the global ocean creates a governance and finance framework that will reshape marine industries and conservation markets.
insights.quote: only around 1% of the high seas are protected

insights.claim: The High Seas Treaty's first Conference of the Parties is scheduled for January 2027, with Hubbard hoping well over 100 countries will be party by then and flagging finance for implementation as the key unresolved question.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The Jan 2027 COP1 and its funding gap are a concrete milestone and risk for the treaty's marine-protection ambitions and related blue-economy finance.
insights.quote: We hope that well over 100 countries will be party to the agreement by COP1
insights.locator: CB: We’ve got almost exactly six months

insights.claim: Swiss glaciers are set to lose an enormous amount of ice, hitting the second-earliest arrival on record of the tipping point known as glacier-loss day.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Early glacier-loss days signal accelerating alpine water-supply and hydropower risk for downstream European economies.

insights.claim: England's Major Oak in Sherwood Forest, which has grown for at least 1,000 years, failed to produce leaves this year after being stressed by a series of hot, dry summers.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The death of a millennium-old tree is a visible marker of cumulative drought stress on established ecosystems, useful as a public-sentiment signal.
